title: Memory Craft — Chinese, Bizarre & Western Mnemonics
description: A complete course on mnemonic technique in three strands: the classical Chinese canon (口诀 rhymed verse, 三字经 chunking, 谐音 homophone pegs, 掐指一算 hand loci, 千字文 no-repeat imagery, 说文解字 etymology decomposition, 永字八法 master exemplar, 歇后语 scene-punchline, 藏头诗 acrostic, 八卦 shape rhyme, 对仗 parallel pairing), the fantastic/bizarre-visual layer (Von Restorff, bizarreness, the six memory-multiplier properties, metaphorAnchor abstraction bridges), and the Western canon (method of loci, link/story, peg systems, Major, PAO, Dominic, number-shape/rhyme, keyword method, mind mapping, dual coding, spaced retrieval). Every topic names its catalog technique id (catalog:<id>) so the pedagogy KG can resolve the underlying evidence. Worked examples draw on the security curriculum domain (kill chain, OSI layers, HTTP status families) for consistency with the red/blue/purple tracks. Pedagogy: foundations first, then encoding families from visual to verbal to numeric to decompositional, ending in integration and spaced retrieval.

modules.title: Foundations — Why Mnemonics Work
modules.objective: Build the mental model everything else stands on: dual coding, chunking, picture superiority, and when a mnemonic is (and isn't) the right tool.

topics.title: Dual Coding & the Picture Superiority Effect
concepts: catalog:dual-coding — Paivio: verbal and imaginal systems are separate but linked; encoding in both gives two retrieval routes.
Picture superiority: pictures are recalled roughly twice as well as words; concreteness is the lever.
Referential links: the point is not the picture, it is the CUE — the image must re-encode the same proposition as the words.
Decorative imagery backfires (seductive detail): every pixel is either a memory write or noise.
key_facts: A fact encoded verbally AND visually has two independent retrieval routes; either can reconstruct the other.
The image must encode the SAME proposition as the words — decoration measurably hurts recall.
Test every image: could the learner reconstruct the fact from image + caption alone?
drills: Take the fact 'HTTP 404 = the server looked and found nothing' and encode it twice: once in words, once as a sketch. Then reconstruct the words from the sketch alone.
Given a decorated diagram of the TCP handshake, cross out every element that doesn't encode a protocol fact. What remains?
common_mistakes: Confusing dual coding with 'learning styles' — everyone benefits from both channels; there are no 'visual learners'.
Adding vivid but irrelevant imagery, which steals encoding from the target fact.

topics.title: Chunking & the Limits of Working Memory
concepts: catalog:chunking — Miller's 7±2: span limits are escaped by grouping items into superordinate units.
Chunks must be meaningful or rhythmically bounded; a chunk is self-checking when it has a fixed frame.
catalog:trisyllabic-chunking — the 三字经 frame: 3-beat groups are near-optimal and audible errors surface instantly.
Rhythm and meter are external retrieval constraints: they narrow candidates so partial recall completes itself.
key_facts: Working memory holds a handful of UNITS — chunking is how experts carry fifty facts in five slots.
Group sizes of 3–4 are optimal for serial recall; the beat makes a malformed chunk instantly audible.
'Stop, Drop, and Roll' survives decades because the 3-beat frame is self-checking, not because the words are special.
drills: Chunk the 7 OSI layers into two rhythmically-bounded groups and recite them as triplets-plus-one; tap the beat while recalling.
Take the 7 kill-chain stages and regroup them into 3 chunks of your own design; justify the boundaries.
common_mistakes: Letting the beat distort the fact — a corrupted item in perfect rhythm is worse than none.
Recalling chunks but losing the ORDER ACROSS chunks; chunk order needs its own cue.

topics.title: When Mnemonics Are the Right Tool
concepts: Mnemonics are a SECONDARY aid for arbitrary material — anything with real logic should be reasoned, not memorized.
The five primitives every technique composes: imagination, association, senses, exaggeration, location.
Three WHY-rules: people beat objects as pegs; take the first association; use soundalikes for the unpicturable.
catalog:retrieval-practice — no encoding survives without retrieval; the chant, palace, or peg only sets up the test.
key_facts: A worked example plus a recall attempt beats a memory trick for anything the learner can reason through.
Every mnemonic in this course is a preset built from five moves: imagination, association, senses, exaggeration, location.
Encoding without spaced retrieval decays — the classical recitation-to-criterion drill WAS the retrieval practice.
drills: Sort ten facts from your current study into 'reason it' vs 'encode it' piles; defend each sort in one line.
For one 'encode it' fact, name which of the five primitives your planned encoding actually uses.
common_mistakes: Reaching for a palace when the material has causal structure that should be taught as a worked example.
Building elaborate encodings and never scheduling a single retrieval attempt.

modules.title: The Fantastic Layer — Bizarre Visual Encoding
modules.objective: Master the visual-encoding engine shared by every image-based technique: Von Restorff distinctiveness, the bizarreness effect, the six memory-multiplier properties, the one-bizarre-element rule, the 千字文 no-repeat law, and metaphorAnchor abstraction bridges.

topics.title: Von Restorff, Bizarreness & Emotional Salience
concepts: Von Restorff (1933): one distinctive item in a homogeneous series is recalled 2–3× better — distinctiveness is relative, not absolute.
Bizarreness effect (McDaniel & Einstein 1986): impossible imagery earns deeper elaborative encoding, ~25–40% better recall.
catalog:bizarreness-exaggeration — exactly ONE odd element, ON the target, against a mundane scene; if everything screams, nothing is distinctive.
Emotional salience: humor and gentle absurdity beat fear/disgust for technical content — affect attaches to the concept.
key_facts: The bizarre element must sit ON the target concept; a weird background is noise.
Exactly ONE bizarre element per scene — budget bizarreness across the whole session.
Prefer object/scale/material violations (a ticket sealed in a jar of cold blue flame); anatomical impossibilities render and recall as mush.
drills: Encode 'HTTP 403 = understood but refused' as one mundane scene with exactly one scale-violation element on the refusal.
Take a peer's scene with three bizarre elements and cut it down to one; explain which one carries the fact.
common_mistakes: Surreal blur: multiple bizarre elements per scene so none is distinctive.
Bizarre background, mundane target — the salience lands on the wrong thing.

topics.title: The Six Memory-Multiplier Properties
concepts: Six axes: novelty, violation, motion implication, scale violation, sensory specificity, self-reference hook.
Score each image ≥4/6; below that, regenerate with one more violating axis.
Serial position: first/last loci score 4/6, MIDDLE loci score 6/6 — the middle is what vanishes (primacy/recency).
Sensory specificity binds detail to the object ('dented brass knuckle'), never to the background.
key_facts: Every strong mnemonic image scores on at least four of six axes: novelty, violation, motion, scale, sensory, self-reference.
The middle of any sequence is where recall dies — spend your highest absurdity budget there.
The best vivid detail RE-ENCODES the fact ('its second hand ticking in ever-widening sweeps' = the interval growing).
drills: Score three of your own study images against the six axes; regenerate the weakest with two more axes.
For a 7-step kill-chain encoding, assign multiplier budgets per position (4/6 endpoints, 6/6 middle) and justify.
common_mistakes: Uniform vividness — every image equally absurd, so the middle steps still vanish.
Free-floating style words ('warm orange tones') instead of object-bound sensory detail.

topics.title: The 千字文 No-Repeat Imagery Law
concepts: catalog:no-repeat-imagery-rule — within one image set, every anchor image must be unique: no shared shape, color family, creature, or action.
千字文 (Thousand Character Classic, ~6th c.): a primer composed of exactly 1,000 non-repeating characters — uniqueness as a compositional law.
Cue overload / fan effect (Anderson 1974): duplicate images make one cue point at two targets; retrieval slows and degrades for both.
Callbacks are the only reuse allowed — and they must MUTATE the quoted image, never clone it.
key_facts: Distinctiveness collapses the moment oddities repeat: two giants, two fires, two melting objects merge into one smeared trace.
Before placing a new image, diff it against everything already placed on shape, color, and action.
The 千字文 enforced this law 1,400 years before the fan effect was measured.
drills: Audit a palace for HTTP methods: PUT already has 'a giant stamping hand' — design POST, GET, DELETE images that share no shape, color, or action with it or each other.
Take a peer's 6-image set, find the two near-duplicates, and mutate one on scale, material, species, or era.
common_mistakes: Near-duplicates slipping in (two 'breaking' actions) and cross-firing at recall.
Over-correction: mutating so far that the element→fact mapping is lost.

topics.title: MetaphorAnchor — Bridging the Abstract
concepts: Abstract concepts cannot be encoded bizarrely directly; build a two-stage bridge: concrete metaphor first, bizarre visualization second.
The metaphorAnchor is the PRE-REVEAL HINT: it must point without giving the answer — it is not the mapping.
The caption is the MAPPING (element → fact), shown after recall; if the mapping can't be stated in one line, the image won't cue.
Keep symbols stable across a session: once 'regularization = tightening strap', never switch it.
key_facts: Two-stage bridge: ABSTRACT → concrete metaphor (metaphorAnchor) → bizarre visualization of the metaphor.
The hint (metaphorAnchor) and the answer (caption) are different artifacts — mixing them ruins both.
A stable symbol vocabulary compounds: each reused stand-in makes the next encoding cheaper.
drills: Bridge 'exponential backoff' end to end: concrete metaphor → one bizarre scene → one-line caption mapping.
Write metaphorAnchors for three abstract security terms (nonce, salt, token) that point without answering.
common_mistakes: A 'metaphor' that is just the definition restated — it gives the answer and kills the recall attempt.
Switching the stand-in for a concept mid-course, fragmenting every trace built on it.

modules.title: Spatial Systems — Palaces & the Body
modules.objective: Encode ordered multi-step material onto spatial scaffolds: the method of loci, the 掐指一算 12-joint hand map, and nested palaces for hierarchies.

topics.title: Method of Loci — the Memory Palace
concepts: catalog:method-of-loci — associate items with ordered locations along a familiar route; recall by mentally walking it.
The locus stays mundane and its phrase is reused VERBATIM; only the placed item carries bizarreness.
One fact per locus; never park two concepts at one locus, never move a concept between loci.
Serial position: densest loci first/last, most vivid in the middle; use ≥1 mutated callback to knit middle loci in.
key_facts: Spatial/navigational memory is a pre-built ordered scaffold — the palace gives recall its order for free.
8–15 loci across 3–5 rooms is the sweet spot for one procedure; a palace is only worth it for ordered multi-step wholes.
Loci training in novices doubled word recall in six weeks (Dresler 2017) — this is the most evidence-backed technique in the course.
drills: Build a 7-room palace for the 7 kill-chain stages, one grotesque image per room, then walk it blind twice.
Convert your commute into a 10-locus route; place the OSI layers on the first 7 loci and recall them in reverse.
common_mistakes: Vague loci ('somewhere in the kitchen') — the phrase must be fixed and reused verbatim.
Reusing a live palace for a new list without letting it decay — lists cross-contaminate.

topics.title: 掐指一算 — the 12-Joint Hand Map
concepts: catalog:hand-loci — the finger joints are a portable, always-available, over-learned spatial order: a body palace needing zero setup.
The canonical map: thumb tip walks the three joints (base → middle → tip) of index, middle, ring, little finger — 12 loci, one fixed traversal direction.
掐指一算 ('pinching the fingers to reckon'): the classical calendrical method, proof the scaffold works at civilizational scale.
Pinching each joint while encoding adds a motor/proprioceptive trace to the spatial one.
key_facts: The hand map holds ordered sets of ≤12 items; beyond that, use a route palace.
Traversal-direction drift is the main failure — fix the direction ONCE and never reverse it.
Joints are less visually distinct than rooms, so the placed images must work harder (higher multiplier budget).
drills: Pinch-encode the 7 OSI layers onto the first 7 joints in stack order; recall by walking the thumb, then backwards from joint 7.
Encode a 12-item security checklist (e.g. the OWASP Top 10 + 2) across the full hand map and recite it with eyes closed.
common_mistakes: Joint confusion — adjacent joints are similar; images migrate unless each is sharply unique (no-repeat law applies).
Reversing the walk mid-recall and corrupting the order.

topics.title: Nested Palaces, Personas & Standalone Hooks
concepts: Nested palace: for hierarchies (ATT&CK tactic → technique → sub-technique), open a room to reveal a sub-palace.
Persona encoding: a NAMED thing becomes a vivid character doing its signature action — people beat objects as pegs.
catalog:peg-system and catalog:link-story-method — chain items by interaction when no fixed route exists; each link is a two-item palace.
A standalone arbitrary fact needs one bizarre association, not a palace — match the structure to the material.
key_facts: Hierarchies encode as palaces-within-palaces: room = category, sub-route = members.
A vivid character (the 'Brad Pitt factor') out-recalls an object for named entities.
The link method's weakness is cascade failure — one broken link loses the tail; palaces don't have this failure mode.
drills: Map the 14 ATT&CK tactics to personas (Initial Access = Cleopatra talking her way through the gate); narrate the walk-through.
Take the STRIDE threat model and encode it twice: once as a link chain, once as 6 rooms; compare recall after 24 hours.
common_mistakes: Building a nested palace when the 'hierarchy' is really a flat list — structure should mirror the material, not impress.
Link chains longer than ~7 items with no positional backup.

modules.title: Verbal & Rhythmic Systems — the Classical Chinese Verse Canon
modules.objective: Encode verbatim-ordered material into rhythm, rhyme, and parallel structure: 口诀 formula verse, 三字经 triplet chunking, 藏头诗 acrostics, 八卦 shape rhymes, 对仗 antithetical pairing, and the Western link/story method.

topics.title: 口诀 — Rhymed Formula Verse
concepts: catalog:rhymed-formula-verse — compress an ordered fact set into a rhymed, metered chant; the cadence forces items back out in order.
九九歌 (nine-nines chant, ~2,200 years old): a 9×9 table recited to fixed cadence becomes a motor routine, not a lookup.
Meter first, then rhyme; fact over rhyme — never distort content to fit the beat.
The documented failure: the chant decouples from the facts — always close with the line→fact mapping.
key_facts: Rhyme and rhythm are external retrieval constraints: each line's end-sound narrows candidates so partial recall completes itself.
If it doesn't chant aloud, it won't stick — verse is an oral technology.
Cap verses at ~30 items; beyond that, chunk into multiple verses or switch to a palace.
drills: Compose a 4-line rhymed verse for the HTTP status families (1xx hold on, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client fault, 5xx server fault) with a MAPPING and RECALL line.
Take a peer's verse and break its meter deliberately; feel how recall collapses — then repair it.
common_mistakes: Verse fluent, meaning lost: reciting the chant without decoding the facts.
Padding lines with abstract filler ('system', 'process', 'various') to fit the meter.

topics.title: 藏头诗 Acrostics & 八卦 Shape Rhymes
concepts: catalog:acrostic-verse — 藏头诗: line-initial characters spell the hidden key; the verse is the carrier, the initials the payload. Predates Western acrostics (ROYGBIV) by ~a millennium.
catalog:shape-rhyme — 八卦 trigram rhymes (乾三连、坤六断…): the verse verbally redraws the figure; reciting reconstructs the picture.
Shape rhyme is dual coding in one artifact: phonological loop replays the description, the mind's eye redraws it.
Acrostic cues discriminate only if initials differ; shared initials kill the cue.
key_facts: Acrostic = double scaffold: narrative order holds the lines, each initial cues one item.
Shape rhyme works only when the figure is simple enough to verbalize losslessly — otherwise use a real diagram.
Both sub-modes drill both directions: verse → list/sketch and list/sketch → verse.
drills: Build an acrostic for the OSI layers ('People Do Need To See Packets Arrive' or your own) and drill initials → layers until fluent.
Write a shape rhyme for the four HTTP status-family figures (1 single pillar, 2 gate pair, 4 broken gate, 5 collapsed gate) and redraw the set from the verse alone.
common_mistakes: Forcing a wrong initial for smoother verse — the payload corrupts silently.
Shape verses too vague to constrain the drawing ('four is a square-ish thing').

topics.title: 对仗 — Antithetical Parallel Pairing
concepts: catalog:antithetical-parallel-pairing — drill contrasts in strict parallel form: matched length, matched structure, antithetical content (对仗; 笠翁对韵: 天对地，雨对风).
The parallel slot is the retrieval cue: recalling one member reinstates the slot its partner must fill (encoding specificity).
Best for opposites, complements, and binary trade-offs; unpairable material forced into antithesis creates false symmetries.
Drill both directions — A→B and B→A — or the pair is only half-learned.
key_facts: Structure, not imagery, is the second code here: the grammar does the mnemonic work.
Relational structure as retrieval cue: antithesis supplies the direction, parallelism supplies the slot.
Centuries of primer use (笠翁对韵) prove the pattern scales to a whole curriculum of pairs.
drills: Pair five security antitheses in strict parallel form (confidentiality/availability, authentication/authorization, encryption/hashing...) and drill them call-and-response.
Take a peer's pair list and find the false symmetry — the pair whose members aren't truly antithetical.
common_mistakes: False symmetry: the parallel form implies a contrast that doesn't exist in the material.
Drilling one direction only, so the reverse lookup fails.

topics.title: Western Verbal Systems — Link/Story & Number Rhymes
concepts: catalog:link-story-method — chain items by absurd interaction: each item acts ON the next; recall replays the story.
catalog:number-rhyme — the Western cousin of 口诀 for digits: one=bun, two=shoe; a fixed peg list with rhyme as the constraint.
East–West convergence: 三字经 triplets and 'Stop, Drop, Roll' are the same 3-beat discovery on two continents.
Cascade failure is the link method's tax: guard long chains by chunking them into scenes of ≤7.
key_facts: The story method and 口诀 verse are the same bet: narrative/rhythmic order substitutes for positional order.
Number-rhyme pegs are pre-memorized once, then reused for any ordered list — the peg is permanent, the images are disposable.
Interaction beats co-location in every verbal system too: 'a bear strangling a router' retrievable, 'a bear next to a router' not.
drills: Link-chain the 7 kill-chain stages into one absurd story (each stage acts on the next); retell it 24 hours later.
Memorize the 1–10 number-rhyme pegs, then hang this week's grocery list on them in under two minutes.
common_mistakes: Links without interaction — items merely adjacent in the story, so the chain breaks.
Reinventing pegs per list instead of learning the fixed rhyme set once.

modules.title: Symbolic & Number Systems
modules.objective: Encode arbitrary digits and symbols: 谐音 homophone pegs, the Major System, number-shape/rhyme, and the person-action systems (PAO, Dominic).

topics.title: 谐音 — Number Homophone Pegs
concepts: catalog:number-homophone-pegs — read digits aloud and map them to soundalike words: 520 → 'I love you', 168 → 'road to prosperity', 88 → 'bye-bye'.
A free peg system: the homophones are already in long-term memory, so the peg list costs nothing to acquire.
Language- and dialect-specific — a mapping that sings in Mandarin is noise in English; use the learner's strongest language.
Homophone drift is the failure mode: a fuzzy pronunciation maps back to the wrong digit.
key_facts: Phonological recoding turns a meaningless digit string into a semantic chunk with existing hooks.
Cantonese license-plate premiums for 8 show the mapping is automatic for a whole population — that's the peg system working at scale.
Drill both directions: number → phrase AND phrase → number.
drills: Encode three port numbers (22, 443, 3389) as homophone phrases in your strongest language, imaged bizarrely; recall the digits from the scenes tomorrow.
Build a personal 0–9 homophone table and use it to memorize a phone number and a CVE id.
common_mistakes: Loose pronunciation that silently swaps a digit on recall.
One-off mappings per number instead of a stable digit→sound table.

topics.title: Major System, Number-Shape & Number-Rhyme
concepts: catalog:major-system — digits map to consonant sounds (0=s/z, 1=t/d, 2=n, 3=m...); vowels are free, so digits become words become images.
catalog:number-shape — digits mapped by visual resemblance (2=swan, 4=sail); the shape-rhyme instinct applied to numerals.
catalog:number-rhyme — digits mapped by rhyme (one=bun); the 口诀 instinct applied to numerals.
Pick ONE digit alphabet and standardize: mixing systems mid-course fragments every trace.
key_facts: The Major System is the 谐音 move made phoneme-exact — consonants fixed, vowels free, so any digit string has a word.
Number-shape is fastest to learn, Major is most general; rhyme sits between.
All three converge on the same endpoint: digits → concrete image → bizarre scene → peg.
drills: Learn the Major consonant table, then encode 443 and 8080 as words and image them interacting at a door.
Encode the same 4-digit PIN via number-shape and via Major; note which recall is faster after one hour and after one day.
common_mistakes: Encoding consonants loosely so the word decodes to wrong digits.
Learning two digit systems at once and cross-firing them.

topics.title: PAO & Dominic — Person-Action-Object at Scale
concepts: catalog:pao-system — digit pairs map to a Person, an Action, and an Object; six digits = one scene (person of pair 1 does action of pair 2 on object of pair 3).
catalog:dominic-system — digit pairs map to initials → people with signature actions; O'Connor's competition system.
People beat objects as pegs (the WHY-rule from foundations) — these systems industrialize that finding.
The 100-image table is the entry fee: fluency with the pegs comes before any long number.
key_facts: PAO compresses 6 digits into ONE interacting scene — the highest digit-density system in the course.
The interaction is the glue: person-acts-on-object is retrievable; three co-located props are not.
Memory-sport evidence (Maguire 2003): these are trained skills, not talents — the brain reorganization follows the practice.
drills: Build a 00–09 PAO starter table (10 people with signature actions and objects), then encode the IPv4 octets of your gateway as one scene.
Encode a 6-digit date (e.g. 070415) as a PAO scene and place it at the first joint of your hand map; recall it in 48 hours.
common_mistakes: Attempting long numbers before the peg table is fluent — decode time kills the trace.
Scenes where person, action, and object don't interact.

modules.title: Decomposition Systems — Terms, Scenes & Exemplars
modules.objective: Encode opaque terms and whole skill categories by taking them apart: 说文解字 etymology decomposition, the keyword method, 歇后语 scene→punchline encoding, and the 永字八法 master exemplar.

topics.title: 说文解字 & the Keyword Method
concepts: catalog:etymology-decomposition — 说文解字 (~100 CE): decompose a term into concrete historical components (roots, radicals), then compose them into one bizarre story image.
catalog:keyword-method — the acoustic cousin: pick a familiar soundalike, image it interacting with the meaning (term → keyword → image → meaning).
Verified splits only: folk etymology encodes a false fact; when the split is dishonest, use a soundalike instead.
Keep component stand-ins stable — one root, one image, reused across every term that shares the root.
key_facts: The decomposition makes the LABEL a retrieval cue for the meaning — the term explains itself.
Keyword gains are large (Raugh & Atkinson: ~88% vs ~28%) but decay fast without spaced retrieval.
The recall path has three links — term→components, components→scene, scene→meaning — and each must be drilled.
drills: Decompose 'cryptography' (kryptós + gráphein) into the cloaked-scribe image and walk the full recall path both directions.
Pick three opaque terms from your current study; decompose two honestly and soundalike the third — justify the choice.
common_mistakes: Invented etymology: a plausible-but-false split that encodes misinformation.
Decomposition without composition: parts listed, no scene, nothing sticks.

topics.title: 歇后语 — Two-Part Scene→Punchline Encoding
concepts: catalog:two-part-allegorical-encoding — a vivid concrete mini-scene, then the punchline meaning: 'a mud bodhisattva crosses the river — it can't even save itself' (泥菩萨过河 — 自身难保).
The scene must ENACT the meaning (dissolving mud = helplessness), not merely co-occur with it.
This is the folk metaphorAnchor: scene first as the pre-reveal hint, punchline as the post-recall mapping.
Drill the pair scene-first: hear the scene, produce the meaning; then reverse.
key_facts: The two-part form packages hint and answer in one saying — the scene points, the punchline lands.
A decorative scene that doesn't enact the meaning cues nothing, however vivid.
歇后语 are a living inventory: thousands of pre-built scene→meaning pairs in the language already.
drills: Compose a 歇后语-style pair for 'defense in depth' — one concrete scene whose physics enact layering, then the punchline.
Take three abstract security principles and write scene→punchline pairs; swap with a peer and test whether the scene alone cues the meaning.
common_mistakes: Scene recalled, punchline forgotten — the halves were never drilled as a pair.
Scenes that illustrate the TOPIC (a castle for security) instead of enacting the PRINCIPLE.

topics.title: 永字八法 — the Master Exemplar
concepts: catalog:master-exemplar — teach a whole skill category through ONE exemplar that contains every case; variants are learned as deltas.
永字八法: the character 永 contains all eight fundamental brush strokes — one character carries the calligraphy curriculum.
Worked-example effect (Sweller): for novices, one well-structured exemplar beats problem-solving from scratch.
The exemplar must genuinely contain every case — a partial exemplar teaches a partial category with false confidence.
key_facts: One integrated trace beats N disconnected ones: each variant encodes as a diff against the exemplar.
Schema abstraction (Gick & Holyoak): mapping variants to a known source case is how transfer actually happens.
Fluency on the exemplar does not guarantee transfer — variants still need interleaved practice.
drills: Choose the '永' of the HTTP request: one canonical request/response pair containing every header class you'll teach; drill it, then derive three variants as explicit diffs.
Find the master exemplar for one skill category in your domain (the canonical nmap scan, the canonical Sigma rule) and enumerate the cases it covers — and any it misses.
common_mistakes: Exemplar gaps: a case the exemplar lacks is never learned, with false confidence.
Exemplar worship: reproducing the canonical case but unable to diff variants against it.

modules.title: Integration — Choosing the Encoding & Keeping It
modules.objective: Select the right encoding for the shape of the material, compose techniques without interference, and lock everything in with spaced retrieval.

topics.title: Choosing the Encoding by the Shape of the Fact
concepts: A NUMBER → homophone pegs / Major / PAO scene. An ORDERED SEQUENCE → palace, hand map, link chain, or 口诀 verse.
A NAMED THING → persona doing its signature action. An ABSTRACT CONCEPT → metaphorAnchor bridge or soundalike.
A VERBATIM LIST → 三字经 triplets, 藏头诗 acrostic, or rhymed verse. A CONTRAST PAIR → 对仗 parallel form. A FIGURE → 八卦 shape rhyme. A SKILL CATEGORY → 永字八法 master exemplar.
When no named recipe fits, COMPOSE from the five primitives rather than forcing a mismatch.
key_facts: The selection table is the course in one line: match the technique to the SHAPE of the material, not to your favorite technique.
One anchor per lesson — don't overload; three techniques on one fact interfere.
Every technique in this course is a preset of the same five moves; the taxonomy is a convenience, not a wall.
drills: Given ten mixed facts (a port number, the kill chain, an ATT&CK technique name, a crypto definition, an HTTP status table, an OSI diagram, a synonym pair...), assign each an encoding family and defend the assignment.
Take one fact you previously encoded badly, diagnose the shape mismatch, and re-encode it with the right family.
common_mistakes: Technique loyalty: forcing every fact through the one system you know best.
Stacking encodings (palace + verse + pegs on the same list) so cues interfere.

topics.title: Spaced Retrieval — Making Encodings Permanent
concepts: catalog:retrieval-practice — effortful recall modifies the trace itself; reciting from cues beats re-reading the verse or re-walking the palace with eyes open.
catalog:spaced-practice — distribute reviews; the expanding schedule is the highest-leverage scheduling decision.
catalog:successive-relearning — recall-to-criterion, drop, re-test later: the classical recitation drill formalized.
catalog:metacognition-calibration — the fluency illusion: a palace that 'feels known' and isn't; only test outcomes are ground truth.
key_facts: The classical recitation-to-criterion culture WAS successive relearning — encoding and retrieval were never separate in the tradition.
Keyword-style gains decay fastest without relearning (Wang, Thomas & Ouellette 1992) — schedule the reviews when you build the image.
The testing effect (Roediger & Karpicke: g ≈ 0.5–0.6 across meta-analyses) is the largest replicated effect in learning science.
drills: Schedule a 4-session spaced plan for one palace built this course (1h, 1d, 3d, 7d) and execute the first two blind.
Take a verse you 'know' and test it from first-beat cues only; log every dropped line and re-encode those harder.
common_mistakes: Re-reading / re-walking instead of recalling — recognition fluency masquerading as memory.
Building new encodings while unreviewed old ones silently decay.

topics.title: Capstone — Assemble Your Personal Memory System
concepts: A working system = one palace route + one hand map + one digit alphabet + one verse habit + a spaced review loop.
catalog:mind-mapping — the pre-encoding step: map the material's structure BEFORE choosing anchors, so the mnemonic mirrors the logic.
The no-repeat law applies across your whole practice: keep a standing inventory of your recurring symbols and stand-ins.
Maintenance beats construction: ten minutes of blind recall per day keeps every palace in the course alive.
key_facts: Fluency is a system property: the pegs, routes, and review loop reinforce each other; isolated tricks decay.
Your stable stand-in vocabulary (built in decomposition-systems) is the compounding asset — guard it.
The end state: given any arbitrary material, you name its shape, pick the encoding, and schedule the first review — in under a minute.
drills: Capstone: take a full topic from the red-team track (e.g. the 14 ATT&CK tactics with 3 techniques each), choose and execute a complete encoding plan (structure map → palaces/personas → pegs → review schedule), and demonstrate blind recall of the whole set.
Write your personal one-page cheat sheet: your palace routes, your digit table, your stand-in vocabulary, your review cadence.
common_mistakes: Collecting techniques without a maintenance loop — a museum of decayed palaces.
Skipping the structure map and memorizing material whose logic should have been taught instead.
